question: Diode lasers are the workhorse of AMO labs. An extended-cavity diode laser (ECDL) in Littrow configuration uses a diffraction grating to narrow the linewidth and tune the wavelength. Which statement correctly describes the role of each component?

options: The diode is the gain medium; the grating's first order feeds back into the diode and the zeroth order is the output beam; PZT on the grating provides fine tuning; temperature provides coarse tuning
The diode is the gain medium; the grating's zeroth order feeds back into the diode and the first order is the output beam; PZT provides coarse tuning and temperature provides fine tuning
The wavelength-selective element is a Fabry-Pérot etalon rather than a diffraction grating; the grating in an ECDL only serves as a mirror
The grating provides feedback in both first and zeroth orders simultaneously; PZT and temperature are interchangeable tuning knobs of equal bandwidth
passive.explanation: ECDL linewidth: feedback from the grating extends the effective cavity length from $\sim 0.3$ mm (diode chip) to $\sim 5$ cm (chip + free space), reducing linewidth from $\sim 100$ MHz (free-running) to $\sim 100$ kHz–1 MHz. Current tuning: $\sim 300$ MHz/mA (fast, $< 1\,\mu$s). PZT tuning: $\sim 300$ MHz/V, bandwidth $\sim 10$ kHz. Temperature tuning: $\sim 10$ GHz/°C (slow, minutes). Typical single-mode output $\sim 1{-}5$ mW. The Littman-Metcalf configuration uses the grating at grazing incidence — narrower linewidth but lower output power.

active.prompt: Match each ECDL parameter to its typical value and physical origin:

pairs.definition: $\sim 100$ MHz (Schawlow-Townes + $1/f$ noise, carrier fluctuations); white frequency noise floor
$\sim 100$ kHz–1 MHz; reduced by external cavity feedback; still limited by acoustic/thermal noise
$< 1$ kHz–10 Hz; limited by vibration of reference cavity; state-of-art: $< 1$ Hz on ultra-stable cavities
$\sim 10{-}20$ GHz — limited by competition between external cavity modes; determined by PZT stroke and current ramp synchronisation

active.explanation: Mode-hop-free tuning: simultaneously ramp PZT (cavity length) and injection current (gain peak). The PZT changes mode spacing and the current shifts the gain curve — when synchronised, the laser slides through frequencies without jumping to a different longitudinal mode. Commercial ECDLs (Toptica DL pro, MOGLabs CEL): typically $> 10$ GHz mode-hop-free range, sub-MHz linewidth.

question: The Pound-Drever-Hall (PDH) technique locks a laser frequency to a high-finesse reference cavity. How is the error signal generated?

options: Phase-modulate the laser with an EOM at $\Omega_m$; the cavity-reflected beat between carrier and sidebands is demodulated at $\Omega_m$ to give an antisymmetric error signal
Detect the cavity-transmitted power directly with a photodiode and feed the DC level back to the laser current — no modulation needed
Amplitude-modulate the laser at the cavity linewidth and demodulate the transmitted intensity to get a side-of-fringe error signal
Beat the laser against a second laser locked to a different cavity and use the heterodyne signal as the error
passive.explanation:
PDH error signal near resonance: $\varepsilon \approx -8\sqrt{P_c P_s}/(P_{m inc}) \cdot (\delta
u/\Delta
u_{m fsr}) \cdot \mathcal{F}/\pi$, where $P_c$ is carrier power, $P_s$ sideband power. Discriminant slope: $D = dV_{m error}/d
u \approx 8\eta\sqrt{P_c P_s}/\Omega_m$ V/Hz. Bandwidth: the PDH signal has bandwidth $\sim\Delta
u_{m cav}$ (cavity linewidth, typically MHz), much wider than side-of-fringe ($\sim \Gamma_{m laser}$). This wide bandwidth enables fast servo corrections — locking to $< 1$ Hz linewidth on ultra-stable cavities.

question: Optical injection locking transfers the frequency and phase of a master laser to a higher-power slave laser. How does the locking range scale with injected power?

options: $\Delta\nu_{\rm lock} \propto \sqrt{P_{\rm master}/P_{\rm slave}}$ — the Adler equation; within this range the slave is phase-coherent with the master
$\Delta\nu_{\rm lock} \propto P_{\rm master}/P_{\rm slave}$ — linear in the injected power ratio with no square root
$\Delta\nu_{\rm lock}$ is independent of injected power; it is set only by the cavity finesse of the slave
Injection locking only works when master and slave wavelengths already agree to better than the slave's free-running linewidth
passive.explanation: Adler equation: $\Delta\omega_{\rm lock} = (\omega_0/2Q_L)\sqrt{P_{\rm inj}/P_{\rm slave}}$ where $Q_L$ is the slave laser $Q$. For a diode laser slave ($Q_L \sim 10^8$, $P_{\rm inj}/P_{\rm slave} \sim 10^{-4}$): $\Delta\nu \sim 100$ MHz — sufficient to pull a free-running slave (linewidth $\sim 10$ MHz) within the master's range. Benefits: slave output power up to 1 W (tapered amplifiers) with master coherence. Must monitor locking via output spectrum or heterodyne beat with master.

question: Saturated absorption spectroscopy is the standard technique for laser frequency stabilisation to an atomic reference. What is the minimum setup required?

options: Counter-propagating pump and probe beams through a room-temperature atomic vapour cell; the probe transmission shows narrow Lamb dips at the true transition frequencies, on top of Doppler-broadened absorption
Co-propagating pump and probe beams through a liquid-nitrogen-cooled vapour cell to suppress Doppler broadening at source
A single beam at 45° to a thermal atomic beam, so the velocity component along the beam is reduced by $\cos 45°$
Two independent lasers locked to each other, one of which serves as the frequency reference for the spectroscopy
passive.explanation: Practical setup: Rb cell at room temperature (vapour pressure already sufficient). Pump beam retroreflected or counter-propagating. Modulation: chop the pump at $\sim 10$ kHz; lock-in detect probe signal. The Lamb dips at transitions and crossover resonances provide $\sim 10{-}30$ MHz wide features. Typical lock bandwidth: $\sim 100$ kHz with a PI controller. Frequency stability: $\sim 100$ kHz long-term (limited by pressure shifts, magnetic fields, temperature). Modulation transfer spectroscopy (MTS) or frequency modulation spectroscopy (FMS) give narrower, dispersive error signals.

question: A tapered amplifier (TA) chip is a semiconductor optical amplifier with a tapered gain region. Which combination of properties accurately describes a typical TA in an AMO lab?

options: 0.5–2 W output from $\sim 10{-}50$ mW seed; ASE pedestal $\sim 10^{-3}$ of total power; elliptical astigmatic output beam; sensitive to back-reflection — an isolator is mandatory
Operates fully without a seed laser — produces clean single-mode output spontaneously once driven above threshold current
Insensitive to back-reflection because the tapered geometry prevents counter-propagating modes from reaching the chip facet
Output is a circular Gaussian beam directly from the chip facet, requiring no anamorphic or cylindrical-lens correction
passive.explanation: TA practical tips: (1) Always maintain seed injection before applying drive current — the TA will lase on ASE without seed and may damage itself or the experiment. (2) Optical isolator (60 dB) between TA output and experiment is mandatory — any reflection into the TA causes instability and catastrophic failure. (3) TA chip lifetime: $\sim 5000$ h at rated current; degrade faster at higher current or temperature. (4) ASE filtering: bandpass filter or reflection from a diffraction grating can reduce ASE from $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ of total power. (5) Mode matching: anamorphic prism pair + spherical lens to circularise the elliptical output ($\sim 4{:}1$ aspect ratio).
